chapters.title: Looking for better ways to win games
chapters.text:
Michael Lewis was fascinated by how some players and executives undervalued by big leagues became some of the most successful individuals in Major League Baseball. He also pondered how the Oakland Athletics (Oakland A’s), who were not financially buoyant, kept winning. During the 2002 season, the New York Yankees boasted a payroll of $126 million. In contrast, the Oakland A’s operated with a budget of approximately $40 million, yet Oakland emerged victorious. The wealthiest teams usually get the best players, so there is a tendency that they will always win, right? But wealthy teams like the Rangers, the Orioles, the Dodgers, and the Mets were failing while the Oakland A’s, who had one of the lowest payrolls in the game, won more regular-season games than any other team — except the Atlanta Braves. In 2000, the Oakland A’s budget was $26 million, but they still won 91 games.

The secret was that the Oakland front office was willing to rethink baseball; the general manager, Billy Beane, sought ways to improve the game. He came up with a science experiment that made them excel.

Professional baseball is about how well you can use what you have.

Michael Lewis introduces us to the Oakland Athletics baseball team and its general manager, Billy Beane. He focuses on the team’s analytical approach to winning with a small budget. Let's discover how Billy Beane’s method knocks it out of the park!

chapters.title: The story of Billy Beane
chapters.text:
As a young man, Billy Beane was a better player than others in every sport he played. He excelled as the football team's quarterback and emerged as the star player on the high school basketball team. Billy Beane’s father, who was not an athlete, taught him baseball using manuals. When Billy entered high school, he ditched the manuals and focused on learning from his coach. In the 1980 draft, the Mets made him their first overall pick.

The Mets only had the highest expectations of Billy Beane. Their head scout, Roger Jongewaard, anticipated he would swiftly progress through the minors and reach the big leagues long before Darryl Strawberry, whom they had also signed in the 1980 draft. The Mets assigned Darryl to the low-level rookie team with high school kids and Billy to the high-level rookie team with college players.

They had prejudices about Billy Beane's abilities, seemingly setting him up for failure. But by the summer of 1982, he had earned a promotion to the Mets' Double-A team in Jackson, Mississippi. On the field, Billy Beane positioned in left field while Darryl Strawberry took his place in right field.

Billy Beane’s major weakness on the ball field was his inability to hit.

Eventually, Billy Beane reached a turning point and decided to pursue a career as an advance scout, traveling ahead of the team to analyze the strengths and weaknesses of opponents. Even though the manager was perplexed that someone would quit as a player to be an advance scout, he hired him anyway. Billy stopped being the guy who should have made it on the field and used his ferocious need to win to become the one making decisions about players.

In 1993, the Oakland A’s general manager Sandy Alderson, touched by the creative enthusiasm Billy used to attack his tasks, brought him in and made him his assistant, scouting for undervalued minor-league players. He handed Billy the pamphlet he had commissioned from Eric Walker on the works of Bill James, which gave Billy an objective view of baseball. His mind finally found an escape hatch to use his learnings.

chapters.title: What makes a baseball player
chapters.text:
Billy Beane’s fascination with baseball statistics revealed a new way to understand the game, going beyond mere observation to a deeper analysis. This approach challenged a traditional belief held by many baseball authorities that player performance could only be assessed visually. Bill James, known for his groundbreaking work on fielding statistics, argued that relying solely on sight was inadequate for evaluating players and games. This raised a significant question: Without complete historical records or visual clarity, how can one accurately identify and appreciate the skills of exceptional fielders?

Bill James introduced a novel statistic known as the “range factor,” which quantified the number of successful plays a fielder executed during each game. Nevertheless, range factors had their limitations. For instance, an outfielder on a team of fly-ball pitchers can easily make more successful plays than an outfielder on a team of sinkerball pitchers.

Baseball statistics are not actual accomplishments of people but a combination of circumstances.

After reading all twelve of Bill James’ abstracts, Billy Beane discovered that he held a false notion of what makes a successful baseball player and realized there were better ways to do things. So, ten years after Bill James stopped writing his abstracts, the Oakland Athletics took the knowledge developed by Bill James and other analysts outside the game, implemented it, and extended the information to inside the game. Of their coveted list of twenty players, they secured thirteen — four pitchers and nine hitters. They selected players previously dismissed by scouts as too short, skinny, heavy, or slow. They chose pitchers whose throwing velocity fell short of the scouts' expectations and batters who lacked formidable power. They even drafted first-round prospects who had never envisioned a selection before the fifteenth round and lower-round prospects who had never anticipated being drafted at all.

Did you know? In 2006, according to CNN, 47% of Americans identified themselves as fans of professional baseball.

chapters.title: Finances don’t win games
chapters.text:
The Oakland Athletics had $40 million to spend on 25 baseball players when their opponent had already spent $126 million on its 25 players and held perhaps another $100 million. As a result, the underprivileged team had to seek economic options — young talents and the overlooked seasoned players available in the market. The wealthy teams had already picked up all the real talent, but against all the odds, the Oakland Athletics stood a chance.

In 1999, the Blue Ribbon Panel Report aimed to investigate whether baseball's prevailing economic structure had led to a problem of competitive inequality within the sport. By July 2000, the panel had reached a verdict: Impoverished teams had minimal prospects for success or survival, and this bleak scenario was detrimental to baseball. A solution was necessary to narrow the gap between well-off and less fortunate teams. If financial resources so determined results in pro-baseball, how did the Oakland Athletics, with one of the lowest payrolls in all of baseball, win so many games?

Some chalked up to sheer luck; others thought they possessed hidden insights or were becoming more efficient. However, the clue is that the players are more replaceable than the team managers believed. With the right formula, the managers could decipher the one attribute most critical to the team’s success that the team could afford to buy.

Replacing a good player is difficult because you might not find another with identical winning skills.

Jason Giambi’s departure from the Oakland Athletics team after the 2001 season left a gaping hole. No other first baseman could match his abilities, and even if there were, the team couldn't afford them. Billy Beane realized that the key wasn't to recreate the individual player but to identify the essential components of Jason Giambi that the team couldn't function without and acquire them at a fraction of the cost of Jason Giambi himself.

After much analysis, the Oakland Athletics’ officials realized they could, in fact, afford to replace him. But with 5,413 in attendance, they lost to the Yankees that year due to the hole they could not fill.

If you have twelve different pitchers, you’ve got to speak twelve different languages. ~ Rick Peterson

chapters.title: An efficient approach to the baseball market
chapters.text:
While playing for the Red Sox, Scott Hatteberg coped with a ruptured nerve in his elbow, which he inadvertently compressed every time he extended his throwing arm. Eventually, he opted for surgery to reposition the nerve to its proper location. However, following the procedure, he found himself incapable of gripping or throwing a baseball. He had to learn how to play from scratch. When the Rockies’ rights to Hatteberg expired in 2002, Paul DePodesta, assistant general manager of the Oakland Athletics, called Hatteberg’s agent to make him an offer.

Hatteberg directed his agent to cut a deal with Oakland, and the moment he signed it, he got a call from Billy Beane, who said he was pleased to have him in the lineup. Hatteberg labored over the most basic task, such as getting into position to receive throws from other infielders. However, a transformation occurred; the more he took the field as a first baseman, the more at ease he became, and the more he wanted the ball thrown at him. He felt like himself again, having fun and making plays people did not expect him to make.

Do not give up on your players easily; look for ways to put them back on track.

It was July 2002, and Billy Beane didn’t need to look at the boards to make connections. He knew which players from other teams he wanted and those in his system that he didn’t. So, he adeptly convinced other teams to acquire his players for more than their apparent value while selling their players for less than they were worth. He had mastered the strategy of capitalizing on the fact that, as bad teams lost hope, their loss of confidence brought the urge to cut costs, leading to them dump their players. As the supply of players rose and their prices fell, Billy acquired players he could never have bought at the beginning of the season.

chapters.title: Spotting diamonds in the rough
chapters.text:
The team transformed from good to exceptional when Billy Beane acquired Ricardo Rincon and Ray Durham. In the past 50 years, only the 2001 Oakland Athletics outperformed the 2002 Oakland Athletics in terms of their second-half record. Chad Bradford, the A’s best pitcher and the most effective relief pitcher in baseball, was the most critical to the team’s success. Before joining Oakland for a nominal cost, Chad was a player who had remained virtually unnoticed by the big leagues.

Top executives in the game attributed Oakland’s win to Billy Beane’s luck with the pitchers. This perception stemmed from Billy's adoption of a distinct mental framework for evaluating Big League Pitchers. Pitchers were more like writers in his mind; they initiated action and established the tone for their games. Pitchers had several ways of achieving their results, and judgment should be based on those effects rather than on their outward appearance or technique. Exceptional pitchers consistently secured outs, and how they achieved this was secondary.

Don’t give room for comparison because every player is different, and each should be viewed as an exceptional case.

The Oakland Athletics were on a 19-game winning streak, and it had become a national news story. They needed to win against the Kansas City Royals to complete a 20-game winning streak. For Billy Beane, it was just another game; he never watched them because the games gave him a subjective emotion, which could be counterproductive.

When others tried to compare Chavez and Jason Giambi, Barry Bonds, and Alex Rodriguez, Billy Beane’s simple response was that baseball players tend to follow established patterns, which are well-documented in the record books. While some players may deviate from their statistical trajectories, within a team of 25, these outliers typically balance each other out. Meaning most of them will conform fairly precisely to his expectations.

chapters.title: The temptation to leave
chapters.text:
The Oakland Athletics had run a low-budget franchise as efficiently as a low-budget business, and no one noticed. All people cared about was how the team fared in the postseason. Just like his players, Billy Beane was underpaid and still needed funds to move forward, so all he could think of was exploiting the market inefficiency by trading himself. John Henry had just acquired the Boston Red Sox and was looking to overhaul his franchise in the image of the Oakland Athletics. He appointed Bill James as Senior Consultant of Baseball Operations, then offered Billy Beane a staggering $12.5 million over five years, the highest-ever compensation for running a baseball team, if he agreed to lead the Boston Red Sox. When it came time for Billy to sign the Red Sox contract, he could not do it, paralyzed as the choice involved himself. He would’ve been the highest-paid general manager in the game’s history. But, now that everyone knew his actual value, Billy Beane didn’t need to prove it anymore.

Satisfaction from impacting other people's lives is more valuable than money.

Since he was not doing it for the love of the Red Sox, Billy Beane rejected the offer and went back to scheming how to get the Oakland Athletics back to the playoffs. Paul DePodesta was ready to support him. He harbored concerns that he and Paul DePodesta might never discover more ingenious methods to build a formidable baseball club with limited financial resources. Nevertheless, he realized their accomplishments might go unnoticed unless they secured a World Series championship.

Even if they won a World Series ring, he thought he would end up like other general managers, celebrated for a day and then forgotten. But he was wrong because he transformed the lives of ballplayers whose hidden virtues the world wouldn’t have seen otherwise. And now, those players on the receiving end kept busy returning the favor.

The pain of looking bad is worse than the pain of making the best move. ~ Pete Palmer

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Billy Beane became the executive vice president of baseball operations and a minority owner of the Oakland Athletics in the 2015 season. He is also a minority shareholder of Barnsley Football Club of England’s English Football League Championship. In addition, he owns AZ Alkmaar of the Eredivisie in the Netherlands. He re-invented baseball and effectively changed the narrative about playing baseball, managing baseball teams, and evaluating players. Although the Oakland A’s wasn’t the wealthiest group in baseball then, they managed to win more regular games than all but one of the other 29 teams.

Although Billy Beane had the opportunity to quit Oakland and manage another team with higher pay, he couldn’t. The impact he was making in the lives of the Oakland athletes meant so much more to him than money, so he stayed back. The management’s unpopular but statically analytical approach to choosing players was the key to their success.

They went for players other teams didn’t want. Billy Beane had an eye for seeing hidden potential in athletes who looked weak at face value. He wasn’t willing to give up on any player but believed that even the wounded Scott Hatteberg that the Red Sox rejected could benefit his team. So, he signed Scott up as soon as possible. Billy Beane eventually became one of the greatest innovators of modern baseball due to the statistical approach he used in recruiting players into the team.

Try this



• List the goals you want to achieve over the next three months and develop strategies to succeed.
• When you fail at one thing, don’t give up. Instead, find better ways to succeed, even changing tactics.

Read it till the end?
